# Generated by ollama (starcoder2)
# Prompt: Create an Envoy proxy configuration file with admin interface settings. Configure the admin address and port for health checks and statistics. Set up access logging and stats endpoints. This is for our Envoy edge proxy deployment.
# Category: envoy_admin_interface_exposed

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ingress-nginx
  namespace: ingress-nginx
  labels:
    app.kubernetes.io/name: ingress-nginx
    app.kubernetes.io/part-of: ingress-nginx
spec:
  externalTrafficPolicy: Local #this is optional and it will set the service to be only accessible from the same Kubernetes cluster as the proxy is running in (i.e. not over a cloud proxy) - which makes more sense for production, as we are on GKE - we do not want to leave traffic open in case we migrate
  type: ClusterIP #it could also have been of the type ExternalIP type so that traffic is accessible from outside of the cluster. This is also optional and it will set the service to be only accessible from the same Kubernetes cluster as the proxy is running in (i.e. not over a cloud proxy) - which makes more sense for production, as we are on GKE
  ports:
    # these two ports are exposed by the ingress-nginx daemonset. The loadbalancer or ingress controller will forward the traffic to it. We will use port 8080 for stats and 9193 (the standard admin port) for healthchecks - note that you can also have a service on another port
    # ingress-controller listens on port 443 in the daemonset
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP
      appProtocol: HTTP
    - name: https
      port: 443
      targetPort: https
      protocol: TCP
      appProtocol: HTTPS
  selector:
    # note that this is ingress-nginx, not kubernetes - it will match with the label in the pod template
    app.kubernetes.io/name: ingress-nginx
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1beta1 #this is the new api, so we will use this in production to get rid of a lot of warnings that come up in gke (due to deprecated things like service type: NodePort). Also it might be possible that this api will be replaced by v1 (no guarantees)
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ingress-nginx #this is for internal communication between the two services. A namespace can have multiple ingresses, and each one must be unique to ensure proper access (in this case we want only one ingress per namespace, so it is a good practice to use that as the ingress name) - but you could also choose to use the whole namespace name in this case
  namespace: ingress-nginx
spec:
  rules:
    # for each rule we define an entry with a host and paths
    # the host is the DNS for the service (i.e. mydomain.com). It is not possible to add wildcards on a specific hostname - you must have a wildcard (like *.mydomain.com) that matches all hosts and then define the actual hostname (in this case ingress-nginx) as an exact match
    # the paths field will be used for more granular access control, that is, only certain paths or services are accessible through the proxy. This would allow you to separate things like authentication, authorization and resource isolation
    - host: ingress-nginx.your-domain
      http:
        # this is the default backend - it will match requests with a path of / (and not a specific path) in our ingress rules section above. It is also the service that handles all requests that do not fit into any path - so if you add another rule, then you would need to define a rule here as well for each different backend service
        # note that this will serve traffic only on port 80 (not https), and you can have multiple paths defined here. This is optional but it is good practice to always have this field there - note also that if you are using a Service as your http backend, then the https one is ignored!
        paths:
          - path: /
            backend:
              serviceName: ingress-nginx #the service name must be in lowercase (and not a string). You could use an internal DNS entry to match both of them. Note also that we did not have a service above in this case for each backend, just a default one!
              servicePort: 80
    - host: test-ingress.your-domain
      http:
        paths:
          # note that for ingress rules we do not set the target port here (the port of the service). It will look into its spec/ports section to find out which port to use when matching the requests to it
          # also in the path we define the path, and then in backend we only specify the name of the service! - this is good practice because it allows us not have to change all our ingress rules if they ever move between namespaces or something like that. Also note that for the defaultBackend - this is just for matching purposes so there should be a service available (not necessarily in this ingress) at the moment, and it could also be external!
          # and we use 9102 instead of 80 here because ingress-nginx listens on port 443 by default. Also note that this is just for internal communication between the ingress and the proxy. Traffic will not go over these ports! The traffic is forwarded to 9102 (standard admin port), 9193(standard health check port) - the same 9193 (standard admin port on the proxy itself), 8443, 9180 and that are exposed by this ingress
          # also note that you should not define a default backend with a host! Otherwise you will get an error. This is a good practice because if you want to do the internal routing by path (i.e. /api, /ui etc...) then a backend service is only available for each unique set of paths. If you want to do more advanced matching based on headers (such as Authorization headers or something) then you cannot define one default backend
          - path: /stats
            backend:
              serviceName: ingress-nginx #remember that we are still using ingress controllers and not a loadbalancer, so this would be an internal DNS lookup. Also note the 9102!
            httpHeaders:
              # here is where we set the header. If you want to set more than one, then you must use the same name - and each entry for each header will have its own key/value pair in the array above (not just a single array)
              - name: kuma-cluster
                value: dev-1
      tls:
        # this is for ssl / https
        - hosts:
            - test-ingress.your-domain
          secretName: ingress-secret
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: ingress-secret
  namespace: ingress-nginx
type: kubernetes.io/tls
data:
  # note that here we are putting the certificate and key into base64 format - which is kind of painful to do by hand! But this way you can easily add more certs with kustomize and not worry about changing all these fields if you ever need to update the cert or key. You will have to make sure that you always use the correct name for the secret (so that it matches exactly in this field above)
  tls.crt: |
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
tls.key: |
    -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
